texts.orig: This text is based on the chapter Domain Theory in the Handbook of Logic in Computer Science , volume 3, edited by S. Abramsky, Dov M. Gabbay, and T. S. E. Maibaum, published by Clarendon Press, Oxford in 1994. While the numbering of all theorems and definitions has been kept the same, we have included comments and corrections which we have received over the years. For ease of reading, small typographical errors have simply been corrected. Where we felt the original text gave a misleading impression, we have included additional explanations, clearly marked as such.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us begin with the problems which gave rise to Domain Theory:
texts.text: Let us begin with the problems which gave rise to Domain Theory:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Least fixpoints as meanings of recursive definitions. Recursive definitions of procedures, data structures and other computational entities abound in programming languages. Indeed, recursion is the basic effective mechanism for describing infinite computational behaviour in finite terms. Given a recursive definition:
texts.text: Least fixpoints as meanings of recursive definitions. Recursive definitions of procedures, data structures and other computational entities abound in programming languages. Indeed, recursion is the basic effective mechanism for describing infinite computational behaviour in finite terms. Given a recursive definition:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can we give a non-circular account of its meaning? Suppose we are working inside some mathematical structure D . We want to find an element d ∈ D such that substituting d for x in (1) yields a valid equation. The right-hand-side of (1) can be read as a function of X , semantically as f : D → D . We can now see that we are asking for an element d ∈ D such that d = f ( d ) -that is, for a fixpoint of f . Moreover, we want a uniform canonical method for constructing such fixpoints for arbitrary structures D and functions f : D → D within our framework. Elementary considerations show that the usual categories of mathematical structures either fail to meet this requirement at all (sets, topological spaces) or meet it in a trivial fashion (groups, vector spaces).
texts.text: How can we give a non-circular account of its meaning? Suppose we are working inside some mathematical structure D . Moreover, we want a uniform canonical method for constructing such fixpoints for arbitrary structures D and functions f : D → D within our framework. Elementary considerations show that the usual categories of mathematical structures either fail to meet this requirement at all (sets, topological spaces) or meet it in a trivial fashion (groups, vector spaces).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Recursive domain equations. Apart from recursive definitions of computational objects, programming languages also abound, explicitly or implicitly, in recursive definitions of datatypes . The classical example is the type-free λ -calculus [Bar84]. To give a mathematical semantics for the λ -calculus is to find a mathematical structure D such that terms of the λ -calculus can be interpreted as elements of D in such a way that application in the calculus is interpreted by function application. Now consider the self-application term λx.xx . By the usual condition for type-compatibility of a function with its argument, we see that if the second occurrence of x in xx has type D , and the whole term xx has type D , then the first occurrence must have, or be construable as having, type [ D -→ D ] . Thus we are led to the requirement that we have

Thus recursive datatypes again lead to a requirement for fixpoints, but now lifted to the functorial level. Again we want such fixpoints to exist uniformly and canonically.

texts.orig: This second requirement is even further beyond the realms of ordinary mathematical experience than the first. Collectively, they call for a novel mathematical theory to serve as a foundation for the semantics of programming languages.
texts.text: This second requirement is even further beyond the realms of ordinary mathematical experience than the first. Collectively, they call for a novel mathematical theory to serve as a foundation for the semantics of programming languages.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A first step towards Domain Theory is the familiar result that every monotone function on a complete lattice, or more generally on a directed-complete partial order with least element, has a least fixpoint. (For an account of the history of this result, see [LNS82].) Some early uses of this result in the context of formal language theory were [Ard60, GR62]. It had also found applications in recursion theory [Kle52, Pla64]. Its application to the semantics of first-order recursion equations and flowcharts was already well-established among Computer Scientists by the end of the 1960's [dBS69, Bek69, Bek71, Par69]. But Domain Theory proper, at least as we understand the term, began in 1969, and was unambiguously the creation of one man, Dana Scott [Sco69, Sco70, Sco71, Sco72, Sco93]. In particular, the following key insights can be identified in his work:
texts.text: A first step towards Domain Theory is the familiar result that every monotone function on a complete lattice, or more generally on a directed-complete partial order with least element, has a least fixpoint. (For an account of the history of this result, see [LNS82].) Some early uses of this result in the context of formal language theory were [Ard60, GR62]. It had also found applications in recursion theory [Kle52, Pla64]. Its application to the semantics of first-order recursion equations and flowcharts was already well-established among Computer Scientists by the end of the 1960's [dBS69, Bek69, Bek71, Par69]. But Domain Theory proper, at least as we understand the term, began in 1969, and was unambiguously the creation of one man, Dana Scott [Sco69, Sco70, Sco71, Sco72, Sco93]. In particular, the following key insights can be identified in his work:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Domains as types. The fact that suitable categories of domains are cartesian closed , and hence give rise to models of typed λ -calculi. More generally, that domains give mathematical meaning to a broad class of data-structuring mechanisms.
texts.text: Domains as types. The fact that suitable categories of domains are cartesian closed , and hence give rise to models of typed λ -calculi. More generally, that domains give mathematical meaning to a broad class of data-structuring mechanisms.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Recursive types. Scott's key construction was a solution to the 'domain equation'
texts.text: Recursive types. Scott's key construction was a solution to the 'domain equation'

texts.label: text
texts.orig: thus giving the first mathematical model of the type-free λ -calculus. This led to a general theory of solutions of recursive domain equations. In conjunction with (1), this showed that domains form a suitable universe for the semantics of programming languages. In this way, Scott provided a mathematical foundation for the work of Christopher Strachey on denotational semantics [MS76, Sto77]. This combination of descriptive richness and a powerful and elegant mathematical theory led to denotational semantics becoming a dominant paradigm in Theoretical Computer Science.
texts.text: thus giving the first mathematical model of the type-free λ -calculus. This led to a general theory of solutions of recursive domain equations. In conjunction with (1), this showed that domains form a suitable universe for the semantics of programming languages. In this way, Scott provided a mathematical foundation for the work of Christopher Strachey on denotational semantics [MS76, Sto77]. This combination of descriptive richness and a powerful and elegant mathematical theory led to denotational semantics becoming a dominant paradigm in Theoretical Computer Science.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Continuity vs. Computability. Continuity is a central pillar of Domain theory. It serves as a qualitative approximation to computability. In other words, for most purposes to detect whether some construction is computationally feasible it is sufficient to check that it is continuous; while continuity is an 'algebraic' condition, which is much easier to handle than computability. In order to give this idea of continuity as a smoothed-out version of computability substance, it is not sufficient to work only with a notion of 'completeness' or 'convergence'; one also needs a notion of approximation , which does justice to the idea that infinite objects are given in some coherent way as limits of their finite approximations. This leads to considering, not arbitrary complete partial orders, but the continuous ones. Indeed, Scott's early work on Domain Theory was seminal to the subsequent extensive development of the theory of continuous lattices, which also drew heavily on ideas from topology, analysis, topological algebra and category theory [GHK + 80].
texts.text: Continuity vs. Computability. Continuity is a central pillar of Domain theory. It serves as a qualitative approximation to computability. In other words, for most purposes to detect whether some construction is computationally feasible it is sufficient to check that it is continuous; while continuity is an 'algebraic' condition, which is much easier to handle than computability. In order to give this idea of continuity as a smoothed-out version of computability substance, it is not sufficient to work only with a notion of 'completeness' or 'convergence'; one also needs a notion of approximation , which does justice to the idea that infinite objects are given in some coherent way as limits of their finite approximations. This leads to considering, not arbitrary complete partial orders, but the continuous ones. Indeed, Scott's early work on Domain Theory was seminal to the subsequent extensive development of the theory of continuous lattices, which also drew heavily on ideas from topology, analysis, topological algebra and category theory [GHK + 80].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Partial information. A natural concomitant of the notion of approximation in domains is that they form the basis of a theory of partial information, which extends the familiar notion of partial function to encompass a whole spectrum of
texts.text: Partial information. A natural concomitant of the notion of approximation in domains is that they form the basis of a theory of partial information, which extends the familiar notion of partial function to encompass a whole spectrum of

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 'degrees of definedness'. This has important applications to the semantics of programming languages, where such multiple degrees of definition play a key role in the analysis of computational notions such as lazy vs. eager evaluation, and call-by-name vs. call-by-value parameter-passing mechanisms for procedures.
texts.text: 'degrees of definedness'. This has important applications to the semantics of programming languages, where such multiple degrees of definition play a key role in the analysis of computational notions such as lazy vs. eager evaluation, and call-by-name vs. call-by-value parameter-passing mechanisms for procedures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: General considerations from recursion theory dictate that partial functions are unavoidable in any discussion of computability. Domain Theory provides an appropriately abstract, structural setting in which these notions can be lifted to higher types, recursive types, etc.
texts.text: General considerations from recursion theory dictate that partial functions are unavoidable in any discussion of computability. Domain Theory provides an appropriately abstract, structural setting in which these notions can be lifted to higher types, recursive types, etc.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2 Our approach
texts.text: 1.2 Our approach

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is a striking fact that, although Domain Theory has been around for a quartercentury, no book-length treatment of it has yet been published. Quite a number of books on semantics of programming languages, incorporating substantial introductions to domain theory as a necessary tool for denotational semantics, have appeared [Sto77, Sch86, Gun92b, Win93]; but there has been no text devoted to the underlying mathematical theory of domains. To make an analogy, it is as if many Calculus textbooks were available, offering presentations of some basic analysis interleaved with its applications in modelling physical and geometrical problems; but no textbook of Real Analysis. Although this Handbook Chapter cannot offer the comprehensive coverage of a full-length textbook, it is nevertheless written in the spirit of a presentation of Real Analysis. That is, we attempt to give a crisp, efficient presentation of the mathematical theory of domains without excursions into applications. We hope that such an account will be found useful by readers wishing to acquire some familiarity with Domain Theory, including those who seek to apply it. Indeed, we believe that the chances for exciting new applications of Domain Theory will be enhanced if more people become aware of the full richness of the mathematical theory.
texts.text: It is a striking fact that, although Domain Theory has been around for a quartercentury, no book-length treatment of it has yet been published. To make an analogy, it is as if many Calculus textbooks were available, offering presentations of some basic analysis interleaved with its applications in modelling physical and geometrical problems; but no textbook of Real Analysis. Although this Handbook Chapter cannot offer the comprehensive coverage of a full-length textbook, it is nevertheless written in the spirit of a presentation of Real Analysis. That is, we attempt to give a crisp, efficient presentation of the mathematical theory of domains without excursions into applications. We hope that such an account will be found useful by readers wishing to acquire some familiarity with Domain Theory, including those who seek to apply it. Indeed, we believe that the chances for exciting new applications of Domain Theory will be enhanced if more people become aware of the full richness of the mathematical theory.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3 Overview
texts.text: 1.3 Overview

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Domains individually
texts.text: Domains individually

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We begin by developing the basic mathematical language of Domain Theory, and then present the central pillars of the theory: convergence and approximation. We put considerable emphasis on bases of continuous domains, and show how the theory can be developed in terms of these. We also give a first presentation of the topological view of Domain Theory, which will be a recurring theme.
texts.text: We begin by developing the basic mathematical language of Domain Theory, and then present the central pillars of the theory: convergence and approximation. We put considerable emphasis on bases of continuous domains, and show how the theory can be developed in terms of these. We also give a first presentation of the topological view of Domain Theory, which will be a recurring theme.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Domains collectively
texts.text: Domains collectively

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We study special classes of maps which play a key role in domain theory: retractions, adjunctions, embeddings and projections. We also look at construction on domains such as products, function spaces, sums and lifting; and at bilimits of directed systems of domains and embeddings.
texts.text: We study special classes of maps which play a key role in domain theory: retractions, adjunctions, embeddings and projections. We also look at construction on domains such as products, function spaces, sums and lifting; and at bilimits of directed systems of domains and embeddings.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Cartesian closed categories of domains
texts.text: Cartesian closed categories of domains

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A particularly important requirement on categories of domains is that they should be cartesian closed (i.e. closed under function spaces). This creates a tension with the requirement for a good theory of approximation for domains, since neither the category CONT of all continuous domains, nor the category ALG of all algebraic domains is cartesian closed. This leads to a non-trivial analysis of necessary and sufficient conditions on domains to ensure closure under function spaces, and striking results on the classification of the maximal cartesian closed full subcategories of CONT and ALG . This material is based on [Jun89, Jun90].
texts.text: A particularly important requirement on categories of domains is that they should be cartesian closed (i.e. closed under function spaces). This creates a tension with the requirement for a good theory of approximation for domains, since neither the category CONT of all continuous domains, nor the category ALG of all algebraic domains is cartesian closed. This leads to a non-trivial analysis of necessary and sufficient conditions on domains to ensure closure under function spaces, and striking results on the classification of the maximal cartesian closed full subcategories of CONT and ALG . This material is based on [Jun89, Jun90].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Recursive domain equations
texts.text: Recursive domain equations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The theory of recursive domain equations is presented. Although this material formed the very starting point of Domain Theory, a full clarification of just what canonicity of solutions means, and how it can be translated into proof principles for reasoning about these canonical solutions, has only emerged over the past two or three years, through the work of Peter Freyd and Andrew Pitts [Fre91, Fre92, Pit93b]. We make extensive use of their insights in our presentation.
texts.text: The theory of recursive domain equations is presented. Although this material formed the very starting point of Domain Theory, a full clarification of just what canonicity of solutions means, and how it can be translated into proof principles for reasoning about these canonical solutions, has only emerged over the past two or three years, through the work of Peter Freyd and Andrew Pitts [Fre91, Fre92, Pit93b]. We make extensive use of their insights in our presentation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Equational theories
texts.text: Equational theories

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We present a general theory of the construction of free algebras for inequational theories over continuous domains. These results, and the underlying constructions in terms of bases, appear to be new. We then apply this general theory to powerdomains and give a comprehensive treatment of the Plotkin, Hoare and Smyth powerdomains. In addition to characterizing these as free algebras for certain inequational theories, we also prove representation theorems which characterize a powerdomain over D as a certain space of subsets of D ; these results make considerable use of topological methods.
texts.text: We present a general theory of the construction of free algebras for inequational theories over continuous domains. These results, and the underlying constructions in terms of bases, appear to be new. We then apply this general theory to powerdomains and give a comprehensive treatment of the Plotkin, Hoare and Smyth powerdomains. In addition to characterizing these as free algebras for certain inequational theories, we also prove representation theorems which characterize a powerdomain over D as a certain space of subsets of D ; these results make considerable use of topological methods.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Domains and logic
texts.text: Domains and logic

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We develop the logical point of view of Domain Theory, in which domains are characterized in terms of their observable properties, and functions in terms of their actions on these properties. The general framework for this is provided by Stone duality; we develop the rudiments of Stone duality in some generality, and then specialize it to domains. Finally, we present 'Domain Theory in Logical Form' [Abr91b], in which a metalanguage of types and terms suitable for denotational semantics is extended with a language of properties, and presented axiomatically as a programming logic in such a way that the lattice of properties over each type is the Stone dual of the domain denoted by that type, and the prime filter of properties which can be proved to hold of a term correspond under Stone duality to the domain element denoted by that term. This yields a systematic way of moving back and forth between the logical and denotational descriptions of some computational situation, each determining the other up to isomorphism.
texts.text: We develop the logical point of view of Domain Theory, in which domains are characterized in terms of their observable properties, and functions in terms of their actions on these properties. The general framework for this is provided by Stone duality; we develop the rudiments of Stone duality in some generality, and then specialize it to domains. Finally, we present 'Domain Theory in Logical Form' [Abr91b], in which a metalanguage of types and terms suitable for denotational semantics is extended with a language of properties, and presented axiomatically as a programming logic in such a way that the lattice of properties over each type is the Stone dual of the domain denoted by that type, and the prime filter of properties which can be proved to hold of a term correspond under Stone duality to the domain element denoted by that term. This yields a systematic way of moving back and forth between the logical and denotational descriptions of some computational situation, each determining the other up to isomorphism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.1. A set P with a binary relation /subsetsqequal is called a partially ordered set or poset if the following holds for all x, y, z ∈ P :
texts.text: Definition 2.1.1. A set P with a binary relation /subsetsqequal is called a partially ordered set or poset if the following holds for all x, y, z ∈ P :

texts.label: code
texts.orig: 1. x /subsetsqequal x (Reflexivity) 2. x /subsetsqequal y ∧ y /subsetsqequal z = ⇒ x /subsetsqequal z (Transitivity) 3. x /subsetsqequal y ∧ y /subsetsqequal x = ⇒ x = y (Antisymmetry)
texts.text: 1. x /subsetsqequal x (Reflexivity) 2. x /subsetsqequal y ∧ y /subsetsqequal z = ⇒ x /subsetsqequal z (Transitivity) 3. x /subsetsqequal y ∧ y /subsetsqequal x = ⇒ x = y (Antisymmetry)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Small finite partially ordered sets can be drawn as line diagrams (Hasse diagrams). Examples are given in Figure 1. We will also allow ourselves to draw infinite posets by showing a finite part which illustrates the building principle. Three examples are given in Figure 2. We prefer the notation /subsetsqequal to the more common ≤ because the order on domains we are studying here often coexists with an otherwise unrelated intrinsic order. The flat and lazy natural numbers from Figure 2 illustrate this.

texts.orig: If we drop antisymmetry from our list of requirements then we get what is known as preorders . This does not change the theory very much. As is easily seen, the subrelation /subsetsqequal ∩ /supersetsqequal is in any case an equivalence relation and if two elements from two equivalence classes x ∈ A,y ∈ B are related by /subsetsqequal , then so is any pair of elements from A and B . We can therefore pass from a preorder to a canonical partially ordered set by taking equivalence classes. Pictorially, the situation then looks as in Figure 3.

Hence we may sum up these considerations with the slogan: Order theory is the study of transitive relations . A common way to extract the order-theoretic content from a relation R is to pass to the transitive closure of R , defined as ⋃ n ∈ N \{ 0 } R n . Ordered sets can be turned upside down:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.2 Notation from order theory
texts.text: 2.1.2 Notation from order theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The following concepts form the core language of order theory.
texts.text: The following concepts form the core language of order theory.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.3. Let ( P, /subsetsqequal ) be an ordered set.
texts.text: Definition 2.1.3. Let ( P, /subsetsqequal ) be an ordered set.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. A subset A of P is an upper set if x ∈ A implies y ∈ A for all y /supersetsqequal x . We denote by ↑ A the set of all elements above some element of A . If no confusion is to be feared then we abbreviate ↑{ x } as ↑ x . The dual notions are lower set and ↓ A .
texts.text: A subset A of P is an upper set if x ∈ A implies y ∈ A for all y /supersetsqequal x . We denote by ↑ A the set of all elements above some element of A . If no confusion is to be feared then we abbreviate ↑{ x } as ↑ x . The dual notions are lower set and ↓ A .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. An element x ∈ P is called an upper bound for a subset A ⊆ P , if x is above every element of A . We often write A /subsetsqequal x in this situation. We denote by ub ( A ) the set of all upper bounds of A . Dually, lb ( A ) denotes the set of lower bounds of A .
texts.text: An element x ∈ P is called an upper bound for a subset A ⊆ P , if x is above every element of A . We often write A /subsetsqequal x in this situation. We denote by ub ( A ) the set of all upper bounds of A . Dually, lb ( A ) denotes the set of lower bounds of A .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. An element x ∈ P is maximal if there is no other element of P above it: ↑ x ∩ P = { x } . Minimal elements are defined dually. For a subset A ⊆ P the minimal elements of ub ( A ) are called minimal upper bounds of A . The set of all minimal upper bounds of A is denoted by mub ( A ) .
texts.text: An element x ∈ P is maximal if there is no other element of P above it: ↑ x ∩ P = { x } . Minimal elements are defined dually. For a subset A ⊆ P the minimal elements of ub ( A ) are called minimal upper bounds of A . The set of all minimal upper bounds of A is denoted by mub ( A ) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. If all elements of P are below a single element x ∈ P , then x is said to be the largest element . The dually defined least element of a poset is also called bottom and is commonly denoted by ⊥ . In the presence of a least element we speak of a pointed poset .
texts.text: If all elements of P are below a single element x ∈ P , then x is said to be the largest element . The dually defined least element of a poset is also called bottom and is commonly denoted by ⊥ . In the presence of a least element we speak of a pointed poset .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. If for a subset A ⊆ P the set of upper bounds has a least element x , then x is called the supremum or join . We write x = ⊔ A in this case. In the other direction we speak of infimum or meet and write x = /bigsqcap A .
texts.text: If for a subset A ⊆ P the set of upper bounds has a least element x , then x is called the supremum or join . We write x = ⊔ A in this case. In the other direction we speak of infimum or meet and write x = /bigsqcap A .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. A partially ordered set P is a /unionsq -semilattice ( /intersectionsq -semilattice ) if the supremum (infimum) for each pair of elements exists. If P is both a /unionsq - and a /intersectionsq -semilattice then P is called a lattice . A lattice is complete if suprema and infima exist for all subsets.
texts.text: A partially ordered set P is a /unionsq -semilattice ( /intersectionsq -semilattice ) if the supremum (infimum) for each pair of elements exists. If P is both a /unionsq - and a /intersectionsq -semilattice then P is called a lattice . A lattice is complete if suprema and infima exist for all subsets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The operations of forming suprema, resp. infima, have a few basic properties which we will use throughout this text without mentioning them further.
texts.text: The operations of forming suprema, resp. infima, have a few basic properties which we will use throughout this text without mentioning them further.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.4. Let P be a poset such that the suprema and infima occurring in the following formulae exist. ( A,B and all A i are subsets of P .)
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.4. Let P be a poset such that the suprema and infima occurring in the following formulae exist. ( A,B and all A i are subsets of P .)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. A ⊆ B implies ⊔ A /subsetsqequal ⊔ B and /bigsqcap A /supersetsqequal /bigsqcap B . 2. ⊔ A = ⊔ ( ↓ A ) and /bigsqcap A = /bigsqcap ( ↑ A ) .
texts.text: A ⊆ B implies ⊔ A /subsetsqequal ⊔ B and /bigsqcap A /supersetsqequal /bigsqcap B . 2. ⊔ A = ⊔ ( ↓ A ) and /bigsqcap A = /bigsqcap ( ↑ A ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. We illustrate order theoretic reasoning with suprema by showing (3). The element ⊔ A is above each element ⊔ A i by (1), so it is an upper bound of the set { ⊔ A i | i ∈ I } . Since ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) is the least upper bound of this set, we have ⊔ A /supersetsqequal ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) . Conversely, each a ∈ A is contained in some A i and therefore below the corresponding ⊔ A i which in turn is below ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) . Hence the right hand side is an upper bound of A and as ⊔ A is the least such, we also have ⊔ A /subsetsqequal ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) .
texts.text: Proof. We illustrate order theoretic reasoning with suprema by showing (3). The element ⊔ A is above each element ⊔ A i by (1), so it is an upper bound of the set { ⊔ A i | i ∈ I } . Since ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) is the least upper bound of this set, we have ⊔ A /supersetsqequal ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) . Conversely, each a ∈ A is contained in some A i and therefore below the corresponding ⊔ A i which in turn is below ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) . Hence the right hand side is an upper bound of A and as ⊔ A is the least such, we also have ⊔ A /subsetsqequal ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. If A = ⋃ i ∈ I A i then ⊔ A = ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) and similarly for the infimum.
texts.text: If A = ⋃ i ∈ I A i then ⊔ A = ⊔ i ∈ I ( ⊔ A i ) and similarly for the infimum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us conclude this subsection by looking at an important family of examples of complete lattices. Suppose X is a set and L is a family of subsets of X . We call L a closure system if it is closed under the formation of intersections, that is, whenever each member of a family ( A i ) i ∈ I belongs to L then so does ⋂ i ∈ I A i . Because we have allowed the index set to be empty, this implies that X is in L . We call the members of L hulls or closed sets . Given an arbitrary subset A of X , one can form ⋂ { B ∈ L | A ⊆ B } . This is the least superset of A which belongs to L and is called the hull or the closure of A .
texts.text: Let us conclude this subsection by looking at an important family of examples of complete lattices. Suppose X is a set and L is a family of subsets of X . We call L a closure system if it is closed under the formation of intersections, that is, whenever each member of a family ( A i ) i ∈ I belongs to L then so does ⋂ i ∈ I A i . Because we have allowed the index set to be empty, this implies that X is in L . We call the members of L hulls or closed sets . Given an arbitrary subset A of X , one can form ⋂ { B ∈ L | A ⊆ B } . This is the least superset of A which belongs to L and is called the hull or the closure of A .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.5. Every closure system is a complete lattice with respect to inclusion.
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.5. Every closure system is a complete lattice with respect to inclusion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Infima are given by intersections and for the supremum one takes the closure of the union.
texts.text: Proof. Infima are given by intersections and for the supremum one takes the closure of the union.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.3 Monotone functions
texts.text: 2.1.3 Monotone functions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.6. Let P and Q be partially ordered sets. A function f : P → Q is called monotone if for all x, y ∈ P with x /subsetsqequal y we also have f ( x ) /subsetsqequal f ( y ) in Q .
texts.text: Definition 2.1.6. Let P and Q be partially ordered sets. A function f : P → Q is called monotone if for all x, y ∈ P with x /subsetsqequal y we also have f ( x ) /subsetsqequal f ( y ) in Q .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 'Monotone' is really an abbreviation for 'monotone order-preserving', but since we have no use for monotone order-reversing maps ( x /subsetsqequal y = ⇒ f ( x ) /supersetsqequal f ( y ) ), we have opted for the shorter expression. Alternative terminology is isotone (vs. antitone ) or the other half of the full expression: order-preserving mapping.
texts.text: 'Monotone' is really an abbreviation for 'monotone order-preserving', but since we have no use for monotone order-reversing maps ( x /subsetsqequal y = ⇒ f ( x ) /supersetsqequal f ( y ) ), we have opted for the shorter expression. Alternative terminology is isotone (vs. antitone ) or the other half of the full expression: order-preserving mapping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The set [ P m -→ Q ] of all monotone functions between two posets, when ordered pointwise (i.e. f /subsetsqequal g if for all x ∈ P , f ( x ) /subsetsqequal g ( x ) ), gives rise to another partially ordered set, the monotone function space between P and Q . The category POSET of posets and monotone maps has pleasing properties, see Exercise 2.3.9(9).
texts.text: The set [ P m -→ Q ] of all monotone functions between two posets, when ordered pointwise (i.e. f /subsetsqequal g if for all x ∈ P , f ( x ) /subsetsqequal g ( x ) ), gives rise to another partially ordered set, the monotone function space between P and Q . The category POSET of posets and monotone maps has pleasing properties, see Exercise 2.3.9(9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let A = { x ∈ L | f ( x ) /subsetsqequal x } and a = /bigsqcap A . For each x ∈ A we have a /subsetsqequal x and f ( a ) /subsetsqequal f ( x ) /subsetsqequal x . Taking the infimum we get f ( a ) /subsetsqequal /bigsqcap f ( A ) /subsetsqequal /bigsqcap A = a and a ∈ A follows. On the other hand, x ∈ A always implies f ( x ) ∈ A by monotonicity. Applying this to a yields f ( a ) ∈ A and hence a /subsetsqequal f ( a ) .
texts.text: Proof. Let A = { x ∈ L | f ( x ) /subsetsqequal x } and a = /bigsqcap A . For each x ∈ A we have a /subsetsqequal x and f ( a ) /subsetsqequal f ( x ) /subsetsqequal x . Taking the infimum we get f ( a ) /subsetsqequal /bigsqcap f ( A ) /subsetsqequal /bigsqcap A = a and a ∈ A follows. On the other hand, x ∈ A always implies f ( x ) ∈ A by monotonicity. Applying this to a yields f ( a ) ∈ A and hence a /subsetsqequal f ( a ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For lattices, the converse is also true: The existence of fixpoints for monotone maps implies completeness. But the proof is much harder and relies on the Axiom of Choice, see [Mar76].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.4 Directed sets
texts.text: 2.1.4 Directed sets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.8. Let P be a poset. A subset A of P is directed , if it is nonempty and each pair of elements of A has an upper bound in A . If a directed set A has a supremum then this is denoted by ↑ A .
texts.text: Definition 2.1.8. Let P be a poset. A subset A of P is directed , if it is nonempty and each pair of elements of A has an upper bound in A . If a directed set A has a supremum then this is denoted by ↑ A .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ⊔ Directed lower sets are called ideals . Ideals of the form ↓ x are called principal . The dual notions are filtered set and ( principal) filter .
texts.text: ⊔ Directed lower sets are called ideals . Ideals of the form ↓ x are called principal . The dual notions are filtered set and ( principal) filter .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simple examples of directed sets are chains . These are non-empty subsets which are totally ordered, i.e. for each pair x, y either x /subsetsqequal y or y /subsetsqequal x holds. The chain of natural numbers with their natural order is particularly simple; subsets of a poset isomorphic to it are usually called ω -chains. Another frequent type of directed set is given by the set of finite subsets of an arbitrary set. Using this and Proposition 2.1.4(3), we get the following useful decomposition of general suprema.
texts.text: Simple examples of directed sets are chains . These are non-empty subsets which are totally ordered, i.e. for each pair x, y either x /subsetsqequal y or y /subsetsqequal x holds. The chain of natural numbers with their natural order is particularly simple; subsets of a poset isomorphic to it are usually called ω -chains. Another frequent type of directed set is given by the set of finite subsets of an arbitrary set. Using this and Proposition 2.1.4(3), we get the following useful decomposition of general suprema.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.9. Let A be a non-empty subset of a /unionsq -semilattice for which ⊔ A exists. Then the join of A can also be written as
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.9. Let A be a non-empty subset of a /unionsq -semilattice for which ⊔ A exists. Then the join of A can also be written as

texts.orig: ⊔ ↑ { ⊔ M | M ⊆ A finite and non-empty } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: General directed sets, on the other hand, may be quite messy and unstructured. Sometimes one can find a well-behaved cofinal subset, such as a chain, where we say that A is cofinal in B , if for all b ∈ B there is an a ∈ A above it. Such a cofinal subset will have the same supremum (if it exists). But cofinal chains do not always exist, as Exercise 2.3.9(6) shows. Still, every directed set may be thought of as being equipped externally with a nice structure as we will now work out.
texts.text: General directed sets, on the other hand, may be quite messy and unstructured. Sometimes one can find a well-behaved cofinal subset, such as a chain, where we say that A is cofinal in B , if for all b ∈ B there is an a ∈ A above it. Such a cofinal subset will have the same supremum (if it exists). But cofinal chains do not always exist, as Exercise 2.3.9(6) shows. Still, every directed set may be thought of as being equipped externally with a nice structure as we will now work out.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.10. A monotone net in a poset P is a monotone function α from a directed set I into P . The set I is called the index set of the net.
texts.text: Definition 2.1.10. A monotone net in a poset P is a monotone function α from a directed set I into P . The set I is called the index set of the net.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let α : I → P be a monotone net. If we are given a monotone function β : J → I , where J is directed and where for all i ∈ I there is j ∈ J with β ( j ) ≥ i , then we call α ◦ β : J → P a subnet of α .
texts.text: Let α : I → P be a monotone net. If we are given a monotone function β : J → I , where J is directed and where for all i ∈ I there is j ∈ J with β ( j ) ≥ i , then we call α ◦ β : J → P a subnet of α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A monotone net α : I → P has a supremum in P , if the set { α ( i ) | i ∈ I } has a supremum in P .
texts.text: A monotone net α : I → P has a supremum in P , if the set { α ( i ) | i ∈ I } has a supremum in P .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Every directed set can be viewed as a monotone net: let the set itself be the index set. On the other hand, the image of a monotone net α : I → P is a directed set in P . So what are nets good for? The answer is given in the following proposition (which seems to have been stated first in [Kra39]).
texts.text: Every directed set can be viewed as a monotone net: let the set itself be the index set. On the other hand, the image of a monotone net α : I → P is a directed set in P . So what are nets good for? The answer is given in the following proposition (which seems to have been stated first in [Kra39]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 2.1.11. Let P be a poset and let α : I → P be a monotone net. Then α has a subnet α ◦ β : J → P , whose index set J is a lattice in which every principal ideal is finite.
texts.text: Lemma 2.1.11. Let P be a poset and let α : I → P be a monotone net. Then α has a subnet α ◦ β : J → P , whose index set J is a lattice in which every principal ideal is finite.

texts.label: text

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.5 Directed-complete partial orders
texts.text: 2.1.5 Directed-complete partial orders

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.13. A poset D in which every directed subset has a supremum we call a directed-complete partial order , or dcpo for short.
texts.text: Definition 2.1.13. A poset D in which every directed subset has a supremum we call a directed-complete partial order , or dcpo for short.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Examples 2.1.14. · Every complete lattice is also a dcpo. Instances of this are powersets, topologies, subgroup lattices, congruence lattices, and, more generally, closure systems. As Proposition 2.1.9 shows, a lattice which is also a dcpo is almost complete. Only a least element may be missing.
texts.text: Examples 2.1.14. · Every complete lattice is also a dcpo. Instances of this are powersets, topologies, subgroup lattices, congruence lattices, and, more generally, closure systems. As Proposition 2.1.9 shows, a lattice which is also a dcpo is almost complete. Only a least element may be missing.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Every finite poset is a dcpo.
texts.text: Every finite poset is a dcpo.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The set of natural numbers with the usual order does not form a dcpo; we have to add a top element as done in Figure 2. In general, it is a difficult problem how to add points to a poset so that it becomes a dcpo. Using Proposition 2.1.15 below, Markowsky has defined such a completion via chains in [Mar76]. Luckily, we need not worry about this problem in domain theory because here we are usually interested in algebraic or continuous dcpo's where a completion is easily defined, see Section 2.2.6 below. The correct formulation of what constitutes a completion, of course, takes also morphisms into account. A general framework is described in [Poi92], Sections 3.3 to 3.6.
texts.text: The set of natural numbers with the usual order does not form a dcpo; we have to add a top element as done in Figure 2. In general, it is a difficult problem how to add points to a poset so that it becomes a dcpo. Using Proposition 2.1.15 below, Markowsky has defined such a completion via chains in [Mar76]. Luckily, we need not worry about this problem in domain theory because here we are usually interested in algebraic or continuous dcpo's where a completion is easily defined, see Section 2.2.6 below. The correct formulation of what constitutes a completion, of course, takes also morphisms into account. A general framework is described in [Poi92], Sections 3.3 to 3.6.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The points of a locale form a dcpo in the specialization order, see [Vic89, Joh82].
texts.text: The points of a locale form a dcpo in the specialization order, see [Vic89, Joh82].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More examples will follow in the next subsection. There we will also discuss the question of whether directed sets or ω -chains should be used to define dcpo's. Arbitrarily long chains have the full power of directed sets (despite Exercise 2.3.9(6)) as the following proposition shows.
texts.text: More examples will follow in the next subsection. There we will also discuss the question of whether directed sets or ω -chains should be used to define dcpo's. Arbitrarily long chains have the full power of directed sets (despite Exercise 2.3.9(6)) as the following proposition shows.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.15. A partially ordered set D is a dcpo if and only if each chain in D has a supremum.
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.15. A partially ordered set D is a dcpo if and only if each chain in D has a supremum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proof, which uses the Axiom of Choice, goes back to a lemma of Iwamura [Iwa44] and can be found in [Mar76].
texts.text: The proof, which uses the Axiom of Choice, goes back to a lemma of Iwamura [Iwa44] and can be found in [Mar76].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.16. A pointed poset P is a dcpo if and only if every monotone map on P has a least fixpoint.
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.16. A pointed poset P is a dcpo if and only if every monotone map on P has a least fixpoint.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.6 Continuous functions
texts.text: 2.1.6 Continuous functions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1.17. Let D and E be dcpo's. A function f : D → E is ( Scott -) continuous if it is monotone and if for each directed subset A of D we have f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) . We denote the set of all continuous functions from D to E , ordered pointwise, by [ D -→ E ] .
texts.text: Definition 2.1.17. Let D and E be dcpo's. A function f : D → E is ( Scott -) continuous if it is monotone and if for each directed subset A of D we have f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) . We denote the set of all continuous functions from D to E , ordered pointwise, by [ D -→ E ] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A function between pointed dcpo's, which preserves the bottom element, is called strict . We denote the space of all continuous strict functions by [ D ⊥ ! -→ E ] .
texts.text: A function between pointed dcpo's, which preserves the bottom element, is called strict . We denote the space of all continuous strict functions by [ D ⊥ ! -→ E ] .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The identity function on a set A is denoted by id A , the constant function with image { x } by c x .
texts.text: The identity function on a set A is denoted by id A , the constant function with image { x } by c x .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The preservation of joins of directed sets is actually enough to define continuous maps. In practice, however, one usually needs to show first that f ( A ) is directed. This is equivalent to monotonicity.
texts.text: The preservation of joins of directed sets is actually enough to define continuous maps. In practice, however, one usually needs to show first that f ( A ) is directed. This is equivalent to monotonicity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.1.18. Let D and E be dcpo's. Then [ D -→ E ] is again a dcpo. Directed suprema in [ D -→ E ] are calculated pointwise.
texts.text: Proposition 2.1.18. Let D and E be dcpo's. Then [ D -→ E ] is again a dcpo. Directed suprema in [ D -→ E ] are calculated pointwise.

texts.orig: Proof. Let F be a directed collection of functions from D to E . Let g : D → E be the function, which is defined by g ( x ) = ⊔ ↑ f ∈ F f ( x ) . Let A ⊆ D be directed.

texts.orig: The class of all dcpo's together with Scott-continuous functions forms a category, which we denote by DCPO . It has strong closure properties as we shall see shortly. For the moment we concentrate on that property of continuous maps which is one of the main reasons for the success of domain theory, namely, that fixpoints can be calculated easily and uniformly.

texts.orig: The least fixpoint operator is the mathematical counterpart of recursive and iterative statements in programming languages. When proving a property of such a statement semantically, one often employs the following proof principle which is known under the name fixpoint induction (see [Ten91] or any other book on denotational semantics). Call a predicate on (i.e. a subset of) a dcpo admissible if it contains ⊥ and is closed under suprema of ω -chains. The following is then easily established:

When proving a property of such a statement semantically, one often employs the following proof principle which is known under the name fixpoint induction (see [Ten91] or any other book on denotational semantics). Call a predicate on (i.e. a subset of) a dcpo admissible if it contains ⊥ and is closed under suprema of ω -chains. The following is then easily established:

texts.orig: Lemma 2.1.20. Let D be a dcpo, P ⊆ D an admissible predicate, and f : D → D a Scott-continuous function. If it is true that f ( x ) satisfies P whenever x satisfies P , then it must be true that fix ( f ) satisfies P .
texts.text: Lemma 2.1.20. Let D be a dcpo, P ⊆ D an admissible predicate, and f : D → D a Scott-continuous function. If it is true that f ( x ) satisfies P whenever x satisfies P , then it must be true that fix ( f ) satisfies P .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Approximation
texts.text: 2.2 Approximation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the last subsection we have explained the kind of limits that domain theory deals with, namely, suprema of directed sets. We could have said much more about these 'convergence spaces' called dcpo's. But the topic can easily become esoteric and lose its connection with computing. For example, the cardinality of dcpo's has not been restricted yet and indeed, we didn't have the tools to sensibly do so (Exercise 2.3.9(18)). We will in this subsection introduce the idea that elements are composed of (or 'approximated by') 'simple' pieces. This will enrich our theory immensely and will also give the desired connection to semantics.
texts.text: In the last subsection we have explained the kind of limits that domain theory deals with, namely, suprema of directed sets. We could have said much more about these 'convergence spaces' called dcpo's. But the topic can easily become esoteric and lose its connection with computing. For example, the cardinality of dcpo's has not been restricted yet and indeed, we didn't have the tools to sensibly do so (Exercise 2.3.9(18)). We will in this subsection introduce the idea that elements are composed of (or 'approximated by') 'simple' pieces. This will enrich our theory immensely and will also give the desired connection to semantics.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.1 The order of approximation
texts.text: 2.2.1 The order of approximation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.2.1. Let x and y be elements of a dcpo D . We say that x approximates y if for all directed subsets A of D , y /subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ A implies x /subsetsqequal a for some a ∈ A . We say that x is compact if it approximates itself.
texts.text: Definition 2.2.1. Let x and y be elements of a dcpo D . We say that x approximates y if for all directed subsets A of D , y /subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ A implies x /subsetsqequal a for some a ∈ A . We say that x is compact if it approximates itself.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduce the following notation for x, y ∈ D and A ⊆ D :
texts.text: We introduce the following notation for x, y ∈ D and A ⊆ D :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: x /lessmuch y ⇔ x approximates y ↓ ↓ x = { y ∈ D | y /lessmuch x } ↑ ↑ x = { y ∈ D | x /lessmuch y } ↑ ↑ A = ⋃ a ∈ A ↑ ↑ a K ( D ) = { x ∈ D | x compact }

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The relation /lessmuch is traditionally called 'way-below relation'. M.B. Smyth introduced the expression 'order of definite refinement' in [Smy86]. Throughout this text we will refer to it as the order of approximation , even though the relation is not reflexive. Other common terminology for 'compact' is finite or isolated . The analogy to finite sets is indeed very strong; however one covers a finite set M by a directed collection ( A i ) i ∈ I of sets, M will always be contained in some A i already.
texts.text: The relation /lessmuch is traditionally called 'way-below relation'. M.B. Smyth introduced the expression 'order of definite refinement' in [Smy86]. Throughout this text we will refer to it as the order of approximation , even though the relation is not reflexive. Other common terminology for 'compact' is finite or isolated . The analogy to finite sets is indeed very strong; however one covers a finite set M by a directed collection ( A i ) i ∈ I of sets, M will always be contained in some A i already.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In general, approximation is not an absolute property of single points. Rather, we could phrase x /lessmuch y as ' x is a lot simpler than y ', which clearly depends on y as much as it depends on x .
texts.text: In general, approximation is not an absolute property of single points. Rather, we could phrase x /lessmuch y as ' x is a lot simpler than y ', which clearly depends on y as much as it depends on x .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An element which is compact approximates every element above it. More generally, we observe the following basic properties of approximation.
texts.text: An element which is compact approximates every element above it. More generally, we observe the following basic properties of approximation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.2. Let D be a dcpo. Then the following is true for all x, x ′ , y, y ′ ∈ D :
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.2. Let D be a dcpo. Then the following is true for all x, x ′ , y, y ′ ∈ D :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 1. x /lessmuch y = ⇒ x /subsetsqequal y ; 2. x ′ /subsetsqequal x /lessmuch y /subsetsqequal y ′ = ⇒ x ′ /lessmuch y ′ .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.2 Bases in dcpo's
texts.text: 2.2.2 Bases in dcpo's

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.2.3. We say that a subset B of a dcpo D is a basis for D , if for every element x of D the set B x = ↓ ↓ x ∩ B contains a directed subset with supremum x . We call elements of B x approximants to x relative to B .
texts.text: Definition 2.2.3. We say that a subset B of a dcpo D is a basis for D , if for every element x of D the set B x = ↓ ↓ x ∩ B contains a directed subset with supremum x . We call elements of B x approximants to x relative to B .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We may think of the rational numbers as a basis for the reals (with a top element added, in order to get a dcpo), but other choices are also possible: dyadic numbers, irrational numbers, etc.
texts.text: We may think of the rational numbers as a basis for the reals (with a top element added, in order to get a dcpo), but other choices are also possible: dyadic numbers, irrational numbers, etc.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.4. Let D be a dcpo with basis B .
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.4. Let D be a dcpo with basis B .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. For every x ∈ D the set B x is directed and x = ⊔ ↑ B x .
texts.text: For every x ∈ D the set B x is directed and x = ⊔ ↑ B x .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. B contains K ( D ) .
texts.text: B contains K ( D ) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Every superset of B is also a basis for D .
texts.text: Every superset of B is also a basis for D .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. (1) It is clear that the join of B x equals x . The point is directedness. From the definition we know there is some directed subset A of B x with ⊔ ↑ A = x . Let now y, y ′ be elements approximating x . There must be elements a, a ′ in A above y, y ′ , respectively. These have an upper bound a ′′ in A , which by definition belongs to B x .
texts.text: Proof. (1) It is clear that the join of B x equals x . The point is directedness. From the definition we know there is some directed subset A of B x with ⊔ ↑ A = x . Let now y, y ′ be elements approximating x . There must be elements a, a ′ in A above y, y ′ , respectively. These have an upper bound a ′′ in A , which by definition belongs to B x .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (2) We have to show that every element c of K ( D ) belongs to B . Indeed, since c = ⊔ ↑ B c there must be an element b ∈ B c above c . All of B c is below c , so b is actually equal to c .
texts.text: (2) We have to show that every element c of K ( D ) belongs to B . Indeed, since c = ⊔ ↑ B c there must be an element b ∈ B c above c . All of B c is below c , so b is actually equal to c .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (3) is immediate from the definition.
texts.text: (3) is immediate from the definition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Corollary 2.2.5. Let D be a dcpo with basis B .
texts.text: Corollary 2.2.5. Let D be a dcpo with basis B .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The largest basis for D is D itself.
texts.text: The largest basis for D is D itself.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. B is the smallest basis for D if and only if B = K ( D ) .
texts.text: B is the smallest basis for D if and only if B = K ( D ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 'only if' part of (2) is not a direct consequence of the preceding proposition. We leave its proof as Exercise 2.3.9(26).
texts.text: The 'only if' part of (2) is not a direct consequence of the preceding proposition. We leave its proof as Exercise 2.3.9(26).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2.3 Continuous and algebraic domains
texts.text: 2.2.3 Continuous and algebraic domains

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.2.6. A dcpo is called continuous or a continuous domain if it has a basis. It is called algebraic or an algebraic domain if it has a basis of compact elements. We say D is ω -continuous if there exists a countable basis and we call it ω -algebraic if K ( D ) is a countable basis.
texts.text: Definition 2.2.6. A dcpo is called continuous or a continuous domain if it has a basis. It is called algebraic or an algebraic domain if it has a basis of compact elements. We say D is ω -continuous if there exists a countable basis and we call it ω -algebraic if K ( D ) is a countable basis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here we are using the word 'domain' for the first time. Indeed, for us a structure only qualifies as a domain if it embodies both a notion of convergence and a notion of approximation.
texts.text: Here we are using the word 'domain' for the first time. Indeed, for us a structure only qualifies as a domain if it embodies both a notion of convergence and a notion of approximation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the light of Proposition 2.2.4 we can reformulate Definition 2.2.6 as follows, avoiding existential quantification.
texts.text: In the light of Proposition 2.2.4 we can reformulate Definition 2.2.6 as follows, avoiding existential quantification.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.7. 1. A dcpo D is continuous if and only if for all x ∈ D , x = ⊔ ↑ ↓ ↓ x holds.
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.7. 1. A dcpo D is continuous if and only if for all x ∈ D , x = ⊔ ↑ ↓ ↓ x holds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The word 'algebraic' points to algebra. Let us make this connection precise.
texts.text: The word 'algebraic' points to algebra. Let us make this connection precise.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. It is algebraic if and only if for all x ∈ D , x = ⊔ ↑ K ( D ) x holds.
texts.text: It is algebraic if and only if for all x ∈ D , x = ⊔ ↑ K ( D ) x holds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.2.8. Aclosure system L (cf. Section 2.1.2) is called inductive , if it is closed under directed union.
texts.text: Definition 2.2.8. Aclosure system L (cf. Section 2.1.2) is called inductive , if it is closed under directed union.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.9. Every inductive closure system L is an algebraic lattice. The compact elements are precisely the finitely generated hulls.
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.9. Every inductive closure system L is an algebraic lattice. The compact elements are precisely the finitely generated hulls.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. If A is the hull of a finite set M and if ( B i ) i ∈ I is a directed family of hulls such that ⊔ ↑ i ∈ I B i = ⋃ i ∈ I B i ⊇ A , then M is already contained in some B i . Hence hulls of finite sets are compact elements in the complete lattice L . On the other hand, every closed set is the directed union of finitely generated hulls, so these form a basis. By Proposition 2.2.4(2), there cannot be any other compact elements.
texts.text: Proof. If A is the hull of a finite set M and if ( B i ) i ∈ I is a directed family of hulls such that ⊔ ↑ i ∈ I B i = ⋃ i ∈ I B i ⊇ A , then M is already contained in some B i . Hence hulls of finite sets are compact elements in the complete lattice L . On the other hand, every closed set is the directed union of finitely generated hulls, so these form a basis. By Proposition 2.2.4(2), there cannot be any other compact elements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given a group, (or, more generally, an algebra in the sense of universal algebra), then there are two canonical inductive closure systems associated with it, the lattice of subgroups (subalgebras) and the lattice of normal subgroups (congruence relations).
texts.text: Given a group, (or, more generally, an algebra in the sense of universal algebra), then there are two canonical inductive closure systems associated with it, the lattice of subgroups (subalgebras) and the lattice of normal subgroups (congruence relations).

texts.orig: Other standard examples of algebraic domains are:
texts.text: Other standard examples of algebraic domains are:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Any set with the discrete order is an algebraic domain. In semantics one usually adds a bottom element (standing for divergence) resulting in so-called flat domains . (The flat natural numbers are shown in Figure 2.) A basis must in either case contain all elements.
texts.text: Any set with the discrete order is an algebraic domain. In semantics one usually adds a bottom element (standing for divergence) resulting in so-called flat domains . (The flat natural numbers are shown in Figure 2.) A basis must in either case contain all elements.

texts.orig: · The set [ X ⇀ Y ] of partial functions between sets X and Y ordered by graph inclusion. Compact elements are those functions which have a finite carrier. It is naturally isomorphic to [ X -→ Y ⊥ ] and to [ X ⊥ ⊥ ! -→ Y ⊥ ] .
texts.text: The set [ X ⇀ Y ] of partial functions between sets X and Y ordered by graph inclusion. Compact elements are those functions which have a finite carrier. It is naturally isomorphic to [ X -→ Y ⊥ ] and to [ X ⊥ ⊥ ! -→ Y ⊥ ] .

texts.orig: · Every algebraic dcpo is also continuous. This follows directly from the definition. The order of approximation is characterized by x /lessmuch y if and only if there exists a compact element c between x and y .
texts.text: Every algebraic dcpo is also continuous. This follows directly from the definition. The order of approximation is characterized by x /lessmuch y if and only if there exists a compact element c between x and y .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The unit interval is a continuous lattice. It plays a central role in the theory of continuous lattices, see [GHK + 80], Chapter IV and in particular Theorem 2.19.
texts.text: The unit interval is a continuous lattice. It plays a central role in the theory of continuous lattices, see [GHK + 80], Chapter IV and in particular Theorem 2.19.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another way of modelling the real numbers in domain theory is to take all closed intervals of finite length and to order them by reversed inclusion. Single element intervals are maximal in this domain and provide a faithful representation of the real line. A countable basis is given by the set of intervals with rational endpoints.
texts.text: Another way of modelling the real numbers in domain theory is to take all closed intervals of finite length and to order them by reversed inclusion. Single element intervals are maximal in this domain and provide a faithful representation of the real line. A countable basis is given by the set of intervals with rational endpoints.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The lattice of open subsets of a sober space X forms a continuous lattice if and only if X is locally compact. Compact Hausdorff spaces are a special case. Here O /lessmuch U holds if and only if there exists a compact set C such that O ⊆ C ⊆ U . This meeting point of topology and domain theory is discussed in detail in [Smy92, Vic89, Joh82, GHK + 80] and will also be addressed in Chapter 7.
texts.text: The lattice of open subsets of a sober space X forms a continuous lattice if and only if X is locally compact. Compact Hausdorff spaces are a special case. Here O /lessmuch U holds if and only if there exists a compact set C such that O ⊆ C ⊆ U . This meeting point of topology and domain theory is discussed in detail in [Smy92, Vic89, Joh82, GHK + 80] and will also be addressed in Chapter 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At this point it may be helpful to give an example of a non-continuous dcpo. The easiest to explain is depicted in Figure 4 (labelled D ). We show that the order of approximation on D is empty. Pairs ( a i , b j ) and ( b i , a j ) cannot belong to the order of approximation because they are not related in the order. Two points a i /subsetsqequal a j in the same 'leg' are still not approximating because ( b n ) n ∈ N is a directed set with supremum above a j but containing no element above a i .
texts.text: At this point it may be helpful to give an example of a non-continuous dcpo. The easiest to explain is depicted in Figure 4 (labelled D ). texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.10. Let D be a continuous domain with basis B and let x and y be elements of D . Then x /subsetsqequal y , B x ⊆ B y and B x ⊆ ↓ y are all equivalent.
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.10. Let D be a continuous domain with basis B and let x and y be elements of D . Then x /subsetsqequal y , B x ⊆ B y and B x ⊆ ↓ y are all equivalent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The form in which we will usually apply this proposition is: x /negationslash/subsetsqequal y implies there exists b ∈ B x with b /negationslash/subsetsqequal y . A picture of this situation is given in Figure 5.
texts.text: The form in which we will usually apply this proposition is: x /negationslash/subsetsqequal y implies there exists b ∈ B x with b /negationslash/subsetsqequal y . A picture of this situation is given in Figure 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the light of Proposition 2.2.10 we can now also give a more intuitive reason why the dcpo D in Figure 4 is not continuous. A natural candidate for a basis in D is the collection of all a i 's and b i 's (certainly, /latticetop doesn't approximate anything). Proposition 2.2.10 expresses the idea that in a continuous domain all information about how elements are related is contained in the basis already. And the fact that ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n = ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N b n = /latticetop holds in D is precisely what is not visible in the would-be basis. Thus, the dcpo should look rather like E in the same figure (which indeed is an algebraic domain).

Thus, the dcpo should look rather like E in the same figure (which indeed is an algebraic domain).

texts.orig: Bases allow us to express the continuity of functions in a form reminiscent of the /epsilon1 -δ definition for real-valued functions.
texts.text: Bases allow us to express the continuity of functions in a form reminiscent of the /epsilon1 -δ definition for real-valued functions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.11. A map f between continuous domains D and E with bases B and C , respectively, is continuous if and only if for each x ∈ D and e ∈ C f ( x ) there exists d ∈ B x with f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e .
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.11. A map f between continuous domains D and E with bases B and C , respectively, is continuous if and only if for each x ∈ D and e ∈ C f ( x ) there exists d ∈ B x with f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. By continuity we have f ( x ) = f ( ⊔ ↑ B x ) = ⊔ ↑ d ∈ B x f ( d ) . Since e approximates f ( x ) , there exists d ∈ B x with f ( d ) /supersetsqequal e . Monotonicity of f then implies f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e .
texts.text: Proof. By continuity we have f ( x ) = f ( ⊔ ↑ B x ) = ⊔ ↑ d ∈ B x f ( d ) . Since e approximates f ( x ) , there exists d ∈ B x with f ( d ) /supersetsqequal e . Monotonicity of f then implies f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the converse we first show monotonicity. Suppose x /subsetsqequal y holds but f ( x ) is not below f ( y ) . By Proposition 2.2.10 there is e ∈ C f ( x ) \↓ f ( y ) and from our assumption we get d ∈ B x such that f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e . Since y belongs to ↑ d this is a contradiction. Now let A be a directed subset of D with x as its join. Monotonicity implies ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) /subsetsqequal f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = f ( x ) . If the converse relation does not hold then we can again choose e ∈ C f ( x ) with e /negationslash/subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) and for some d ∈ B x we have f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e . Since d approximates x , some a ∈ A is above d and we get ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) /supersetsqequal f ( a ) /supersetsqequal f ( d ) /supersetsqequal e contradicting our choice of e .
texts.text: For the converse we first show monotonicity. Suppose x /subsetsqequal y holds but f ( x ) is not below f ( y ) . By Proposition 2.2.10 there is e ∈ C f ( x ) \↓ f ( y ) and from our assumption we get d ∈ B x such that f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e . Since y belongs to ↑ d this is a contradiction. Now let A be a directed subset of D with x as its join. Monotonicity implies ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) /subsetsqequal f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = f ( x ) . If the converse relation does not hold then we can again choose e ∈ C f ( x ) with e /negationslash/subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) and for some d ∈ B x we have f ( ↑ d ) ⊆ ↑ e . Since d approximates x , some a ∈ A is above d and we get ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) /supersetsqequal f ( a ) /supersetsqequal f ( d ) /supersetsqequal e contradicting our choice of e .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.13. If a dcpo D has a countable basis then every directed subset of D contains an ω -chain with the same supremum.
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.13. If a dcpo D has a countable basis then every directed subset of D contains an ω -chain with the same supremum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This raises the question whether one shouldn't build up the whole theory using ω -chains. The basic definitions then read: An ω -ccpo is a poset in which every ω -chain has a supremum. A function is ω -continuous if it preserves joins of ω -chains. An element x is ω -approximating y if ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n /supersetsqequal y implies a n /supersetsqequal x for some n ∈ N . An ω -ccpo is continuous if there is a countable subset B such that every element is the join of an ω -chain of elements from B ω -approximating it. Similarly for algebraicity. (This is the approach adopted in [Plo81], for example.) The main point about these definitions is the countability of the basis. It ensures that they are in complete harmony with our set-up, because we can show:

A function is ω -continuous if it preserves joins of ω -chains. An element x is ω -approximating y if ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n /supersetsqequal y implies a n /supersetsqequal x for some n ∈ N . An ω -ccpo is continuous if there is a countable subset B such that every element is the join of an ω -chain of elements from B ω -approximating it. Similarly for algebraicity. (This is the approach adopted in [Plo81], for example.) The main point about these definitions is the countability of the basis. It ensures that they are in complete harmony with our set-up, because we can show:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposition 2.2.14. 1. Every continuous ω -ccpo is a continuous dcpo.
texts.text: Proposition 2.2.14. 1. Every continuous ω -ccpo is a continuous dcpo.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Every algebraic ω -ccpo is an algebraic dcpo.
texts.text: Every algebraic ω -ccpo is an algebraic dcpo.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Every ω -continuous map between continuous ω -ccpo's is continuous.
texts.text: Every ω -continuous map between continuous ω -ccpo's is continuous.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. (1) Let ( b n ) n ∈ N be an enumeration of a basis B for D . We first show that the continuous ω -ccpo D is directed-complete, so let A be a directed subset of D . Let B ′ be the set of basis elements which are below some element of A and, for simplicity, assume that B = B ′ . We construct an ω -chain in A as follows: let a 0 be an element of A which is above b 0 . Then let b n 1 be the first basis element not below a 0 . It must be below some a ′ 1 ∈ A and we set a 1 to be an upper bound of a 0 and a ′ 1 in A . We proceed by induction. It does not follow that the resulting chain ( a n ) n ∈ N is cofinal in A but it is true that its supremum is also the supremum of A , because both subsets of D dominate the same set of basis elements.
texts.text: Proof. (1) Let ( b n ) n ∈ N be an enumeration of a basis B for D . We first show that the continuous ω -ccpo D is directed-complete, so let A be a directed subset of D . Let B ′ be the set of basis elements which are below some element of A and, for simplicity, assume that B = B ′ . We construct an ω -chain in A as follows: let a 0 be an element of A which is above b 0 . Then let b n 1 be the first basis element not below a 0 . It must be below some a ′ 1 ∈ A and we set a 1 to be an upper bound of a 0 and a ′ 1 in A . We proceed by induction. It does not follow that the resulting chain ( a n ) n ∈ N is cofinal in A but it is true that its supremum is also the supremum of A , because both subsets of D dominate the same set of basis elements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This construction also shows that ω -approximation is the same as approximation in a continuous ω -ccpo. The same basis B may then be used to show that D is a continuous domain. (The directedness of the sets B x follows as in Proposition 2.2.4(1).)
texts.text: This construction also shows that ω -approximation is the same as approximation in a continuous ω -ccpo. The same basis B may then be used to show that D is a continuous domain. (The directedness of the sets B x follows as in Proposition 2.2.4(1).)

texts.orig: (2) follows from the proof of (1), so it remains to show (3). Monotonicity of the function f is implied in the definition of ω -continuity. Therefore a directed set A ⊆ D is mapped onto a directed set in E and also f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) /supersetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) holds. Let ( a n ) n ∈ N be an ω -chain in A with ⊔ ↑ A = ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n , as constructed in the proof of (1). Then we have f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = f ( ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n ) = ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N f ( a n ) /subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) .

Therefore a directed set A ⊆ D is mapped onto a directed set in E and also f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) /supersetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) holds. Then we have f ( ⊔ ↑ A ) = f ( ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N a n ) = ⊔ ↑ n ∈ N f ( a n ) /subsetsqequal ⊔ ↑ f ( A ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If we drop the crucial assumption about the countability of the basis then the two theories bifurcate and, in our opinion, the theory based on ω -chains becomes rather bizarre. To give just one illustration, observe that simple objects, such as powersets, may fail to be algebraic domains. There remains the question, however, whether in the realm of a mathematical theory of computation one should start with ω -chains. Arguments in favor of this approach point to pedagogy and foundations. The pedagogical aspect is somewhat weakened by the fact that even in a continuous ω -ccpo the sets ↓ ↓ x happen to be directed. Glossing over this fact would tend to mislead the student. In our eyes, the right middle ground for a course on domain theory, then, would be to start with ω -chains and motivations from semantics and then at some point (probably where the ideal completion of a poset is discussed) to switch to directed sets as the more general concept. This suggestion is hardly original. It is in direct analogy with the way students are introduced to topological concepts.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Turning to foundations, we feel that the necessity to choose chains where directed subsets are naturally available (such as in function spaces) and thus to rely on the Axiom of Choice without need, is a serious stain on this approach. To take foundational questions seriously implies a much deeper re-working of the theory: some pointers to the literature will be found in Section 8.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: sented as Proposition 2.2.7(1)). We nevertheless preferred this approach to the concept of approximation for three reasons. Firstly, the standard definition does not allow the restriction of the size of continuous domains. In this respect not the cardinality of a domain but the minimal cardinality of a basis is of interest. Secondly, we wanted to point out the strong analogy between algebraic and continuous domains. And, indeed, the proofs we have given so far for continuous domains specialize directly to the algebraic case if one replaces ' B ' by ' K ( D ) ' throughout. Thus far at least, proofs for algebraic domains alone would not be any shorter. And, thirdly, we wanted to stress the idea of approximation by elements which are (for whatever reason) simpler than others. Such a notion of simplicity does often exist for continuous domains (such as rational vs. real numbers), even though its justification is not purely order-theoretical (see 8.1.1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: algebraic vs. continuous. This brings up the question of why one bothers with continuous domains at all. There are two important reasons but they depend on definitions introduced later in this text. The first is the simplification of the mathematical theory of domains stemming from the possibility of freely using retracts (see Theorem 3.1.4 below). The second is the observation that in algebraic domains two fundamental concepts of domain theory essentially coincide, namely, that of a Scott-open set and that of a compact saturated set. We find it pedagogically advantageous to be able to distinguish between the two.
texts.text: algebraic vs. continuous. This brings up the question of why one bothers with continuous domains at all. There are two important reasons but they depend on definitions introduced later in this text. The first is the simplification of the mathematical theory of domains stemming from the possibility of freely using retracts (see Theorem 3.1.4 below). The second is the observation that in algebraic domains two fundamental concepts of domain theory essentially coincide, namely, that of a Scott-open set and that of a compact saturated set. We find it pedagogically advantageous to be able to distinguish between the two.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: continuous dcpo vs. continuous domain. It is presently common practice to start a paper in semantics or domain theory by defining the subclass of dcpo's of interest and then assigning the name 'domain' to these structures. We fully agree with this custom of using 'domain' as a generic name. In this article, however, we will study a full range of possible definitions, the most general of which is that of a dcpo. We have nevertheless avoided calling these domains. For us, 'domain' refers to both ideas essential to the theory, namely, the idea of convergence and the idea of approximation.
